name: Honda NSX (NA1)
reliability: 10/10 for a supercar — this is the car that proved an exotic could be as dependable as a Civic. The all-aluminum C30A is famously durable, and Honda engineered the NSX to be driven every day. The caveats are ownership costs, not fragility: the timing belt and water pump are a scheduled major service, some early manual gearboxes suffered a second-gear snap-ring failure, and aging electronics and climate parts need care. Values have gone stratospheric, so provenance and originality matter enormously.
cost_to_own: Insurance: High (appreciating exotic) · Maintenance ~$1,500+/yr · Timing belt + water pump every ~7 years/90k is a four-figure job · Values are very high and still climbing — this is a blue-chip collector Honda
popular_mods: Suspension refresh with OEM-spec or period-correct parts — bushings and dampers age
A tasteful exhaust to wake up the C30A (many owners keep it stock for value)
Refreshed wheels/tires in the correct sizes
Honest take: the NSX has crossed into collector territory — originality is worth far more than any modification, so preserve rather than personalize

generations.NA1 (1991–1996): The original — 3.0L C30A (270 hp with the 5-speed), pop-up headlights, and a Senna-honed aluminum chassis. The 4-speed automatic detuned it to 252 hp. The purest expression of the everyday-supercar idea.
generations.NSX-T (1995+): A removable targa roof panel became the volume body style from 1995 on — slightly heavier but hugely popular in the US.
generations.NA2 (1997–2005): Sold in the US as the Acura NSX — a larger 3.2L C32B (290 hp) with a 6-speed manual, and fixed headlights from 2002. See the Acura NSX (NA1/NA2 era) entry for that car.
generations.NSX-R (JDM): The stripped, track-honed, Japan-only homologation special — the collector's grail.

common_issues: Timing belt + water pump is a scheduled, expensive major service — verify when it was last done
Second-gear snap-ring failure on some early (1991–1994) manual transmissions — a known, documented issue
Aging climate control, electronics, and rubber seals — typical of any 30-year-old exotic
Clutch wear on driven cars; the pop-up headlight motors can fail
Sun-faded interiors and cracked dashboards on unprotected cars
maintenance_tips: Do the timing belt and water pump on the time interval, not just mileage — this is non-negotiable
On early manuals, confirm whether the second-gear snap-ring update has been performed
Use quality 10W-30 and let the C30A warm up before revving
Buy on service history and originality — a documented, unmodified car is worth the premium
Store it well: sun and damp are the enemies of the interior and electronics
